data.title: Veradigm: You Can't Fall Off The Floor
data.description:
During the 1995 Berkshire Annual Meeting, Warren Buffett mentioned:

People feel better when they're on the 2nd floor that just came [up] from 1 than they do when they're on the 99th floor coming down from 100.

This is basically the core of the investment thesis for Veradigm Inc. (MDRX). We are dealing with a company in a horrible situation due to mismanagement and a series of awful decisions, which caused the stock to be delisted from Nasdaq and traded at its lowest prices since 2003. While the stock is currently valued at its worst, and we are standing at the first floor, I believe the underlying business is still intact, and the current management seems to be capable of turning the ship around and putting us in the elevator by relisting the stock on the Nasdaq by the end of 2026. This could be the main short-term catalyst, but not the only one.

Background

This is an overview of the major events that led to the current situation:

  • Mar 2022: Veradigm, Allscripts at the time, sold its hospital EHR business to Constellation Software (CNSWF). This created a much leaner business focusing on the ambulatory market with higher margins. However, the sale was also a catalyst to reveal the accounting issues in the financial statements.
  • Feb 2023: Management disclosed around $20M in revenue recognition errors since the 3rd quarter of 2021 (last 6 quarters at the time).
  • Nov 2023: It was disclosed that the issue had affected all 2021 and 2022 financial statements due to some duplicate transactions and internal control failures. The revenue overstatement estimate was also increased from $20M to $40M. Additionally, the company received a notice of delisting from Nasdaq.
  • Dec 2023: CEO and CFO were forced to step down and replaced by Shin-Yin Ho as interim CEO and Lee Westerfield as interim CFO.
  • Feb 2024: Veradigm was delisted from Nasdaq and started to trade on the OTC market.
  • Mar 2024: To add insult to injury, the new management decided to spend $140M to acquire an AI startup.
  • May 2024: Shin-Yin Ho was replaced by Tom Langan as a new interim CEO. Additionally, rather than dealing with the main issue, the company stated that they are going to explore strategic alternatives.
  • Jan 2025: Exploration of strategic alternatives was concluded. 30 parties were initially engaged with 5 preliminary non-binding interests and no final bid.
  • Feb 2025: Two new board members, Vinit Asar and Louis Silverman, were appointed.
  • March 2025: Restated financial statements by the end of 2022 were filed. Furthermore, the BOD was reshuffled again, and a completely different management team compared to 2023 was formed.
  • August 2025: Don Trigg, ex-president of Cerner (now Oracle Healthcare), was appointed as CEO.
  • Feb 2026: SEC investigation was concluded without any enforcement actions against Veradigm.
  • April 2026: It was announced that a new CFO, Christian Greyenbuhl, will be taking over the CFO role on May 11. Financial statements for fiscal years of 2023 and 2024 are expected to be filed a day before the transition.

While these events show why the stock is trading at its lowest levels in more than 20 years, I believe none of them materially impacted the underlying business. Additionally, the quality of BOD has significantly improved compared to 2023. The process of strategic review is indicative of the existing interest and potential of the business. The timing probably was the main cause of the failure of that process. Who is willing to buy a business with no clear financial statements?

Where are we now?

During the first earnings call after his appointment, Don Trigg outlined three near-term focus points:

  1. Becoming current with SEC filings
  2. Improving operational efficiency and profitability
  3. Reigniting growth

After filing the 2023 and 2024 financial statements in May, the main focus will be getting current and relisting the stock on Nasdaq. As the CEO mentioned in the latest earnings call:

So I think we're trying to be pretty intentional in terms of laying out the order of operations. We've got to get current. We've got to be -- have conviction that our systems and process support stay current and then apply to relist shortly thereafter. So that's sort of the sequential dimension of it. And as I said in my remarks, we want to make sure that we're moving not only with the right pace, but the right level of accuracy. So that's the framework, one. Two, we absolutely remain focused on getting current in 2026.

In January, a new ERP system was launched to modernize the IT systems and also help the process of becoming and staying current.

To increase efficiency, Veradigm decreased its workforce by 15%. They also closed five offices, including the Chicago HQ, to consolidate operations in two offices in Pune, India, and Raleigh, NC. Additionally, the CEO announced the discontinuation of six low-revenue products to focus on high-growth bets.

For boosting growth, the CEO has focused on extracting more value from the existing customers. One area he indicated as having growth potential is integrated billing. While current penetration is less than 5%, the focus is getting more aligned with the clients to encourage them to use Veradigm's financial engine. The launch of a new AI-enabled application for Veradigm RCM in January is a step towards this goal.

Another area of growth is data monetization. Veradigm launched a new real-world data survey solution in February that could be a valuable source of data for life science companies.

Valuation

Before focusing on valuation, I think it is important to understand why the underlying business is intact. Lee Westerfield did a good job illustrating it:

With all of the accounting remediations and IT implementations, it's easy to lose sight of the fact that Veradigm operates attractive businesses. My message to you continues to be this, our fundamentals are sound.

How so? First, our recurring revenue. Nearly 80% of our revenue is recurring generated from an installed base of EHR and health technology solutions. And looking ahead, new solutions coming to market and initiatives to retain and expand our existing customer base should reignite our future growth.

Second, our operating profitability reflects our mix of software revenue and services. This year, under our competitive fitness program, actions are underway to improve efficiency and contribute to profitability in future years.

Finally, we remain soundly capitalized. We are net cash positive. Our leverage is light, and we have ample cash on hand with additional available liquidity for the road ahead. These are the qualities I see anchoring our fundamentals, a firm foundation to renew Veradigm.

The recurring revenue side of the business is sticky with high switching costs. Veradigm is currently the fifth largest player in the ambulatory EHR market with around 3.6% market share. This market is highly fragmented and expected to grow at 5.5% CAGR.

Gathering all available information from conference calls and unaudited financial statements, these are the estimated values:

Net cash has decreased due to the acquisition of ScienceIO in 2024 and some non-recurring items in 2025. Veradigm paid $159M for debt refinancing activities and $62M for non-typical transactions like legal fees, severance, consulting, and accounting fees. Additionally, there was around $20M of cash inflow due to non-recurring investment activities. On the other hand, the business generated around $60M of operating cash flow and $30M of FCF during 2025. Given that EBITDA was historically 30% higher than operating cash flow, the estimated EBITDA for 2025 should be around $80M. Therefore, the stock is currently traded at less than 5.5x EV/EBITDA.

Considering the multiples of the comparable public companies and previous private transactions, 5.5x seems depressed.

I think NextGen and Waystar (WAY) are better representatives for Veradigm due to the similarities in their underlying businesses. However, even considering the current EV/EBITDA of TruBridge at around 7.7x for Veradigm would result in a market cap of around $660M (~ $6/share). This can offer around a 38% return without considering any of the current initiatives to decrease costs and improve efficiency and profitability. I believe just by becoming current with the SEC filings, that is highly possible. Then, the relisting combined with a successful turnaround story can shoot up the stock price further. If the management will be able to grow EBITDA to $120M, which is still lower than the 2022 figure, the stock price could easily be doubled even without multiple expansion.

Conclusion

I think the worst is behind us, and Veradigm seems to be standing at the elevator door and ready to jump into the 2nd or even 3rd floor. This can nicely reward the investors, and that is why I think the stock is a buy. The main risk is probably the prolongation of the relisting process.

data.title: Dallas Fed Manufacturing: Mixed Perceptions In April
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Dallas Fed released its Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey (TMOS) for April. The general business activity index fell 2.1 points to -2.3, indicating slightly worsening business conditions.

Texas manufacturing output growth accelerated in April, according to business executives responding to the Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey. The production index, a key measure of state manufacturing conditions, jumped 12 points to 19.0, a reading suggestive of an above-average pace of output expansion.

Other measures of manufacturing activity also showed signs of faster growth this month. The capacity utilization index moved up 13 points to 19.8, the new orders index increased four points to 9.9 and the shipments index rose 13 points to 15.0.

Perceptions of broader business conditions were mixed in April. The general business activity index fell two points to -2.3. However, the company outlook index rebounded into positive territory, rising seven points to 3.0. The outlook uncertainty index fell eight points to 17.9.

Employment continued to be flat, but workweeks were slightly longer in April. The employment index was unchanged at -0.9, with the near-zero reading indicating no change in payrolls from March. The hours worked index moved up to 4.0 from 0.9.

Price pressures increased while wage pressures were little changed this month. The finished goods prices index jumped nine points to 27.6, its highest level since July 2022, while the raw materials prices index increased to 37.0 from 32.7. The wages and benefits index was relatively unchanged at 24.8.

Press release

Background on the Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey (TMOS)

Monthly data for this indicator only dates back to 2004, so it is difficult to see the full potential of this indicator without several business cycles of data. Nevertheless, it is an interesting and important regional manufacturing indicator.

The TMOS is a monthly survey of 100 Texas manufacturers that provides an assessment of the state's factory activity. The survey asks firms whether output, employment, orders, prices, and other indicators have increased, decreased, or remained unchanged over the previous month. Results are aggregated into balance indexes where negative readings indicate contractions while positive ones indicate expansion.

The Dallas Fed on the importance of Texas Manufacturing:

Texas is important to the nation’s manufacturing output. The state produced $296 billion in manufactured goods in 2023, roughly 11 percent of the country’s manufacturing output. Texas ranks second behind California in factory production and first as an exporter of manufactured goods.

Texas turns out a large share of the country’s production of petroleum and coal products, reflecting the significance of the region’s refining industry. Texas also produces over 13 percent of the nation’s chemical products and over 10 percent of nonmetallic mineral products, such as brick, glass and cement.

Here is a snapshot of the complete TMOS.

Texas Manufacturing Survey Future Outlook

The next chart is an overlay of the general business activity index and the future outlook index - the outlook six months ahead. Future general business activity expectations rose to 14.1 this month.

Expectations are for increased manufacturing activity six months from now. The future production index held steady at 34.6, and the future general business activity index rose four points to 14.1. Other indexes of future manufacturing activity remained in positive territory and increased.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).
